rationale: This page provides detailed information on the Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Rules 2025, including implementation timelines, obligations of data fiduciaries, and citizen rights. It also introduces the Criminal Law Reform Acts, specifically focusing on the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 and its key changes from the IPC 1860.
cleanedText:
7.1.1. DIGITAL PERSONAL DATA PROTECTION (DPDP) RULES, 2025 Implementation timeline: 18-month phased compliance period for organizations to adjust their systems and adopt responsible data practices. Key Provisions of the rules * Obligations on Data Fiduciaries * Issue clear, standalone consent notices with itemised data details and withdrawal mechanism. * Erase personal data once purpose is served, unless legally required to retain it. * Processing logs must be retained for minimum 1 year, unless extended by law. * Notify personal data breaches promptly to affected individuals with remedial steps. * Publish contact details of designated officer/DPO on website or app. * Respond within 90 days to requests for access, correction, updation or erasure. * Children’s data protection through verifiable parental/guardian consent. Consent exemption allowed for essential services like healthcare, education, and child safety. * Data Protection Board of India (DPBI) * Comprises 4 members with a dedicated portal and mobile app for complaint filing and tracking. * Other Provisions * Section 44(3) of the DPDP Act, 2023 Revises Section 8(1)(j) of the RTI Act: By exempting disclosure of information which relates to personal information. The blanket exemption and the vague definition of personal information provides discretion to public authorities. * Enhanced obligations on Significant Data Fiduciaries (SDFs), including periodic Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) and independent audits every 12 months. * Consent Managers must be companies based in India. * Special safeguards for persons with disabilities unable to make legal decisions. * Any personal data processed by a Data Fiduciary may be transferred outside the territory of India subject to the restriction by the Central Government. Rights of citizens under DPDP Rules * Right to Nominate Another Person to exercise their data rights on their behalf. * Right to Give or Refuse Consent, includes withdrawal of consent at any time. * Right to Know How personal Data is Used, includes information on what data has been collected and why it has been collected. * Right to Access Personal Data held by a Data Fiduciary. * Right to Correct and update Personal Data. * Right to Erase Personal Data in certain situations. 7.2. CRIMINAL LAW REFORM ACTS Why in the news? Three new Criminal Laws which came into effect on 1st July 2024 has completed one year. 7.2.1. BHARATIYA NYAYA SANHITA 2023 It replaces the Indian Penal Code (IPC), 1860, the principal law on criminal offences in India. | Aspect | Key Points | | :--- | :--- | | Background | • Before IPC 1860: Laws included Parliamentary Charters, EIC regulations, Hindu & Muslim law, and customary laws.
• Draft Penal Code (1837) prepared under T.B. Macaulay.
• IPC enacted in 1860 after the 1857 Revolt. | | Punishments | • Introduces community service as punishment for petty offences. | | Sexual Offences | • Age for gang rape victim classified as major raised from 16 to 18 years.
• Criminalises sexual intercourse obtained through deceit or false promise. |
